type: SURVEY
files: tiktoken/core.py
note: Adding transformers param to encode() method: accepts single callable or list of callables (str->str), applied sequentially to text before core BPE encoding call

type: SURVEY
files: tiktoken/core.py
note: My feature: add optional return_positions=False param to Encoding.encode() in tiktoken/core.py. When True, encode() returns a tuple (tokens, positions) where positions[i] is the starting character index of tokens[i] in the original text. Only construct I touch is the encode() method body/signature.

type: DRAFT
files: tiktoken/core.py
region: encode() method
code:     def encode(
        self,
        text: str,
        *,
        allowed_special: Literal["all"] | AbstractSet[str] = set(),  # noqa: B006
        disallowed_special: Literal["all"] | Collection[str] = "all",
        transformers: Callable[[str], str] | Sequence[Callable[[str], str]] | None = None,
        return_positions: bool = False,
    ) -> list[int] | tuple[list[int], list[int]]:
        """Encodes a string into tokens.

        Special tokens are artificial tokens used to unlock capabilities from a model,
        such as fill-in-the-middle. So we want to be careful about accidentally encoding special
        tokens, since they can be used to trick a model into doing something we don't want it to do.

        Hence, by default, encode will raise an error if it encounters text that corresponds
        to a special token. This can be controlled on a per-token level using the `allowed_special`
        and `disallowed_special` parameters. In particular:
        - Setting `disallowed_special` to () will prevent this function from raising errors and
          cause all text corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as natural text.
        - Setting `allowed_special` to "all" will cause this function to treat all text
          corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as special tokens.

        `transformers` accepts a single callable or a sequence of callables, each taking a str
        and returning a str; they are applied in order to `text` before encoding.

        If `return_positions` is True, returns a tuple `(tokens, positions)` where `positions[i]`
        is the starting character index of `tokens[i]` in the (possibly transformed) text.

        ```
        >>> enc.encode("hello world")
        [31373, 995]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special={"<|endoftext|>"})
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special="all")
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>")
        # Raises ValueError
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", disallowed_special=())
        [27, 91, 437, 1659, 5239, 91, 29]
        ```
        """
        if transformers is not None:
            if callable(transformers):
                transformers = [transformers]
            for transformer in transformers:
                text = transformer(text)

        if allowed_special == "all":
            allowed_special = self.special_tokens_set
        if disallowed_special == "all":
            disallowed_special = self.special_tokens_set - allowed_special
        if disallowed_special:
            if not isinstance(disallowed_special, frozenset):
                disallowed_special = frozenset(disallowed_special)
            if match := _special_token_regex(disallowed_special).search(text):
                raise_disallowed_special_token(match.group())

        try:
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)
        except UnicodeEncodeError:
            # BPE operates on bytes, but the regex operates on unicode. If we pass a str that is
            # invalid UTF-8 to Rust, it will rightfully complain. Here we do a quick and dirty
            # fixup for any surrogate pairs that may have sneaked their way into the text.
            # Technically, this introduces a place where encode + decode doesn't roundtrip a Python
            # string, but given that this is input we want to support, maybe that's okay.
            # Also we use errors="replace" to handle weird things like lone surrogates.
            text = text.encode("utf-16", "surrogatepass").decode("utf-16", "replace")
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)

        if return_positions:
            _, offsets = self.decode_with_offsets(tokens)
            return tokens, offsets

        return tokens
note: Merged encode(): transformers applied first to text, then existing special-token handling, then return_positions uses decode_with_offsets for positions. Please confirm position computation approach matches your intent, or propose your own offsets logic and I'll adopt it verbatim.

type: DRAFT
files: tiktoken/core.py
region: Encoding.encode() method
code:     def encode(
        self,
        text: str,
        *,
        allowed_special: Literal["all"] | AbstractSet[str] = set(),  # noqa: B006
        disallowed_special: Literal["all"] | Collection[str] = "all",
        return_positions: bool = False,
        transformers: Callable[[str], str] | Sequence[Callable[[str], str]] | None = None,
    ) -> list[int] | tuple[list[int], list[int]]:
        """Encodes a string into tokens.

        Special tokens are artificial tokens used to unlock capabilities from a model,
        such as fill-in-the-middle. So we want to be careful about accidentally encoding special
        tokens, since they can be used to trick a model into doing something we don't want it to do.

        Hence, by default, encode will raise an error if it encounters text that corresponds
        to a special token. This can be controlled on a per-token level using the `allowed_special`
        and `disallowed_special` parameters. In particular:
        - Setting `disallowed_special` to () will prevent this function from raising errors and
          cause all text corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as natural text.
        - Setting `allowed_special` to "all" will cause this function to treat all text
          corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as special tokens.

        If `transformers` is provided, it is a callable (or sequence of callables) of the form
        `str -> str` that is applied to `text` in order before encoding.

        If `return_positions` is True, this returns a tuple `(tokens, positions)` where
        `positions[i]` is the starting character index of `tokens[i]` in the text that was
        actually encoded (i.e. after any `transformers` have been applied).

        ```
        >>> enc.encode("hello world")
        [31373, 995]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special={"<|endoftext|>"})
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special="all")
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>")
        # Raises ValueError
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", disallowed_special=())
        [27, 91, 437, 1659, 5239, 91, 29]
        ```
        """
        if transformers is not None:
            if callable(transformers):
                transformers = [transformers]
            for transformer in transformers:
                text = transformer(text)

        if allowed_special == "all":
            allowed_special = self.special_tokens_set
        if disallowed_special == "all":
            disallowed_special = self.special_tokens_set - allowed_special
        if disallowed_special:
            if not isinstance(disallowed_special, frozenset):
                disallowed_special = frozenset(disallowed_special)
            if match := _special_token_regex(disallowed_special).search(text):
                raise_disallowed_special_token(match.group())

        try:
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)
        except UnicodeEncodeError:
            # BPE operates on bytes, but the regex operates on unicode. If we pass a str that is
            # invalid UTF-8 to Rust, it will rightfully complain. Here we do a quick and dirty
            # fixup for any surrogate pairs that may have sneaked their way into the text.
            # Technically, this introduces a place where encode + decode doesn't roundtrip a Python
            # string, but given that this is input we want to support, maybe that's okay.
            # Also we use errors="replace" to handle weird things like lone surrogates.
            text = text.encode("utf-16", "surrogatepass").decode("utf-16", "replace")
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)

        if return_positions:
            positions = []
            cum_bytes = b""
            for token in tokens:
                positions.append(len(cum_bytes.decode("utf-8", errors="ignore")))
                cum_bytes += self.decode_single_token_bytes(token)
            return tokens, positions

        return tokens
note: Merged encode(): added return_positions (mine) + transformers (yours, applied first to text before BPE encode). Also need to add Callable to the typing import line at top of core.py. Please compare and confirm or send back diffs.

code:     def encode(
        self,
        text: str,
        *,
        allowed_special: Literal["all"] | AbstractSet[str] = set(),  # noqa: B006

        return_positions: bool = False,
        transformers: Callable[[str], str] | Sequence[Callable[[str], str]] | None = None,
    ) -> list[int] | tuple[list[int], list[int]]:
        """Encodes a string into tokens.

        Special tokens are artificial tokens used to unlock capabilities from a model,
        such as fill-in-the-middle. So we want to be careful about accidentally encoding special
        tokens, since they can be used to trick a model into doing something we don't want it to do.

        Hence, by default, encode will raise an error if it encounters text that corresponds
        to a special token. This can be controlled on a per-token level using the `allowed_special`
        and `disallowed_special` parameters. In particular:
        - Setting `disallowed_special` to () will prevent this function from raising errors and
          cause all text corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as natural text.
        - Setting `allowed_special` to "all" will cause this function to treat all text
          corresponding to special tokens to be encoded as special tokens.

        If `transformers` is provided, it is a callable (or sequence of callables) of the form
        `str -> str` that is applied to `text` in order before encoding.

        If `return_positions` is True, this returns a tuple `(tokens, positions)` where
        `positions[i]` is the starting character index of `tokens[i]` in the text that was
        actually encoded (i.e. after any `transformers` have been applied).

        ```
        >>> enc.encode("hello world")
        [31373, 995]

        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", allowed_special="all")
        [50256]
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>")
        # Raises ValueError
        >>> enc.encode("<|endoftext|>", disallowed_special=())
        [27, 91, 437, 1659, 5239, 91, 29]
        ```
        """
        if transformers is not None:
            if callable(transformers):
                transformers = [transformers]
            for transformer in transformers:
                text = transformer(text)

        if allowed_special == "all":
            allowed_special = self.special_tokens_set
        if disallowed_special == "all":
            disallowed_special = self.special_tokens_set - allowed_special

            if not isinstance(disallowed_special, frozenset):
                disallowed_special = frozenset(disallowed_special)
            if match := _special_token_regex(disallowed_special).search(text):
                raise_disallowed_special_token(match.group())

        try:
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)
        except UnicodeEncodeError:
            # BPE operates on bytes, but the regex operates on unicode. If we pass a str that is
            # invalid UTF-8 to Rust, it will rightfully complain. Here we do a quick and dirty
            # fixup for any surrogate pairs that may have sneaked their way into the text.

            # string, but given that this is input we want to support, maybe that's okay.
            # Also we use errors="replace" to handle weird things like lone surrogates.
            text = text.encode("utf-16", "surrogatepass").decode("utf-16", "replace")
            tokens = self._core_bpe.encode(text, allowed_special)

        if return_positions:
            positions = []
            cum_bytes = b""
            for token in tokens:
                positions.append(len(cum_bytes.decode("utf-8", errors="ignore")))
                cum_bytes += self.decode_single_token_bytes(token)
            return tokens, positions

        return tokens
note: Agreed verbatim. Also need Callable added to the typing import at top of core.py: from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, AbstractSet, Callable, Collection, Literal, NoReturn, Sequence -- please confirm this exact import line too.

        self,

            _, offsets = self.decode_with_offsets(tokens)
            return tokens, offsets
